content: 3B rows on DSE with a 99.999% SLA is no joke. Curious what your p99 tail latency looks like under write bursts — teams I work with in similar positions have cut node count by 60%+ without touching a line of application code. Worth a quick exchange?

Subject: Your payments ledger — a node count comparison worth 15 minutes

Saw your work scaling a payments ledger on DataStax Enterprise at 99.999% uptime — that's a hard constraint to build around.

We've been working with fintech teams running similar workloads who've moved to ScyllaDB. The pattern is consistent: same CQL queries, same drivers, same SSTables — but 60-70% fewer nodes and p99 latency cut roughly in half under burst traffic.

No rewrite. The application doesn't know it moved.

If you're ever stress-testing alternatives or just want to see the benchmark data side by side, happy to share. No pitch deck — just the numbers.

[Name] @ ScyllaDB

Running player state on Astra DB across 3 regions is a solid architecture. If your team ever evaluates alternatives, the CQL compatibility story is surprisingly clean — same drivers, same queries, zero application changes. Happy to share what that looks like in practice.

Subject: Player state migration — what 'zero application changes' actually means

Your matchmaking and player state setup on Astra DB across 3 regions looks well thought out. Migrating a stack like that usually feels risky — which is why most teams don't, even when the economics argue for it.

ScyllaDB is fully CQL-compatible: same query syntax, same drivers (DataStax Java/Python drivers work unchanged), same SSTable format for existing data. We've seen gaming teams complete a full migration in a single sprint with no application code changes.

The main thing that moves is the node count — usually down by 3-4x — and the p99 latency under concurrent game session load.

If a low-friction evaluation is interesting to you, I can walk you through exactly what it looks like. Takes about 20 minutes.

[Name] @ ScyllaDB

Your post about re-evaluating the DataStax dependency resonated — a lot of teams are asking the same questions right now. Happy to share what we're seeing from companies that have already made the switch, if useful.

Subject: DataStax renewal timing — what teams are doing right now

Your Cassandra cluster being the biggest cost center in your infra is a real problem, and IBM's first round of post-acquisition renewals has made it worse for most teams.

A handful of streaming companies have moved to ScyllaDB in the last 6 months specifically because the IBM acquisition changed their vendor risk calculus. The migration is technically straightforward — CQL-compatible, same drivers, no application changes — and the cost reduction is meaningful at your scale.

I won't pretend the timing isn't relevant: teams that evaluate before their next renewal have more leverage than those who wait.

Worth 20 minutes to see the numbers? Happy to pull comps from similar streaming workloads.

[Name] @ ScyllaDB

Board questions about long-term vendor strategy on your DataStax stack are coming up more often right now — you're not alone. The open-source alternative with no IBM dependency is a conversation more engineering leaders are having. Happy to share what that looks like at your scale.

Subject: Vendor strategy on your real-time data stack — the open-source option

Board questions about long-term vendor exposure on a DataStax-backed stack are coming up more often since the IBM acquisition. It's a reasonable thing to pressure-test.

ScyllaDB is Apache 2.0 licensed, community-governed, and not owned by a cloud provider. For teams running real-time order routing at scale, that's become a meaningful part of the evaluation — not just the performance numbers.

The technical migration from DataStax to ScyllaDB is straightforward: CQL-compatible, same drivers, existing data files transfer without conversion. The operational risk is low.

If it's useful context for your vendor review, I can put together a one-pager on how other logistics teams have framed this decision. No commitment needed.

[Name] @ ScyllaDB
